coredata.dc:title: Highly stable yellow-emitting fluorescent film based on graphene quantum dots for white laser-emitting devices
coredata.dc:creator: He, Pinyi
Yang, Su
Liu, Xinghua
Yang, Yongzhen
Zheng, Jingxia
Liu, Xuguang
Xu, Bingshe
coredata.dc:description: Green-emitting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a high crystallization degree were prepared by a two-step solvothermal method. Owing to the co-doping of nitrogen and oxygen, carbonyl and amide groups were introduced into GQDs. The quantum yield (QY) of GQD solution is up to 47.09%, which is higher than that of other GQDs synthesized by top-down methods. Since GQD solution exhibits concentration-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties, green, yellow, and orange emissions were realized by adjusting GQD concentration. A high concentration (no less than 6.0 mg mL−1) GQD solution was mixed with KH-792 to form a yellow-emitting fluorescent film with an excellent fluorescence and thermal stability, which was used as color converters and combined with 405 nm laser diodes (LDs) to realize the devices with white laser-emitting. The Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinate of the laser device is (0.34, 0.39), the correlated color temperature (CCT) is 5288 K, and the color rendering index (CRI) is 70. This work provides an advanced way for the development of laser fluorescent materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent film
Laser diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent film and LD devices

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of GQDs (Insets are HRTEM image and the corresponding size distribution histograms of GQDs), (b) AFM image of GQDs (Inset is corresponding height profile of GQDs).
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of GQDs.
(a) Full-scan XPS spectra of GO and GQDs, (b–d) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s of GQDs.
FTIR spectra of GO and GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of GQDs, (b) Emission spectra of GQD solution under different excitation wavelengths (inset is the photographs of GQD solution under sunlight and UV lamp).
(a) QY measurement, (b) Evolution of PL intensity under UV light for 60 min, and (c) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of GQDs.

(a) Emission spectra of GQD solutions with concentrations of 1.5, 3.0, 4.5, and 6.0 mg mL−1 at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm; (b) Emission spectra of 6.0 mg mL−1 GQD solution at different excitation wavelengths, inset: photograph of GQD solution under sunlight and UV lamp; (c) Emission spectra of GQD fluorescent film at different excitation wavelengths, inset: photograph of GQD fluorescent film under sunlight and UV lamp.

(a) TG curve of GQD fluorescent film, (b) Evolution of PL intensity of GQD fluorescent film under UV light for 60 min, (c) Temperature dependent spectra of GQD fluorescent film under excitation at 405 nm, (d) Normalized PL intensity of GQD fluorescent film as a function of temperature.
(a) Emission spectra of white LD under different working voltages, inset is the photograph of the working white LD, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram for white LD.
Synthesis of GQD fluorescent film and its application in white-light laser devices.

head.ce:title: Highly stable yellow-emitting fluorescent film based on graphene quantum dots for white laser-emitting devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Carbon Materials, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong, 030600, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of New Carbon Materials, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong, 030600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green-emitting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a high crystallization degree were prepared by a two-step solvothermal method. Owing to the co-doping of nitrogen and oxygen, carbonyl and amide groups were introduced into GQDs. The quantum yield (QY) of GQD solution is up to 47.09%, which is higher than that of other GQDs synthesized by top-down methods. Since GQD solution exhibits concentration-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties, green, yellow, and orange emissions were realized by adjusting GQD concentration. A high concentration (no less than 6.0 mg mL−1) GQD solution was mixed with KH-792 to form a yellow-emitting fluorescent film with an excellent fluorescence and thermal stability, which was used as color converters and combined with 405 nm laser diodes (LDs) to realize the devices with white laser-emitting. The Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinate of the laser device is (0.34, 0.39), the correlated color temperature (CCT) is 5288 K, and the color rendering index (CRI) is 70. This work provides an advanced way for the development of laser fluorescent materials.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the solid-state light sources used in illumination are mainly light-emitting diodes (LEDs). However, LEDs have the problem of “efficiency droop” at high input current density, which makes LEDs difficult to realize high brightness white light, severely restricting the development of LEDs in high-power solid-state lighting [1,2]. Therefore, researchers have gradually focused their attention on white light sources based on laser diodes (LDs). The luminous efficiency of LDs is thousands of times that of LEDs, and there is no phenomenon of “efficiency droop”. Moreover, LDs have the advantages of small emitting area, long lifetime, and low beam divergence, thus becoming a strong competitor in the field of solid-state lighting [3].

There are two methods to realize white laser illumination. One is to use multiple LDs with different emission wavelengths, and the other is to use a single LD to excite color converters [4,5]. The first method requires multiple LDs to be used together, and the optical path needs to be designed to form white light, which has a high cost and complex process. The second method only uses an LD combined with color converters, which is cost-effective and easy to be industrialized. However, owing to the high energy and concentrated beam of LDs, the laser light source generates a lot of heat when irradiating the surface of the color converters, which causes the rapid decadence of color converters and then the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the color converters matched with LDs must have high thermal stability [6]. Currently, the color converters used in LDs are mainly rare earth doped phosphors with good stability and mature synthesis technology [7,8]. However, the high price of rare earth, shortage of resources, and non-renewability make it urgent to develop an advanced phosphor with high thermal stability to replace rare earth phosphors.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of fluorescent material, which have the advantages of excellent photoluminescence, high stability, good biocompatibility, low cost, and low toxicity [9–11]. They have received an increasing attention since 2006 [12]. In recent years, the researchers have revealed the potential of CDs as the optical gain media for LDs. Zhang et al. used citric acid and silane coupling agent (A-2120) to prepare silanized CDs with a QY of 68%, which was dispersed in epoxy resin as an optical gain medium to realize laser emission [13]. Qu et al. prepared green-emitting CDs with a QY of 36% and realized laser emission by using ethanol solution of CDs as gain medium [14]. Yuan et al. synthesized three types of CDs with blue, green, and red emissions and used them as a gain medium to achieve laser emission [15].

With the development of the research on CDs as optical gain media, using CDs as color converters for white LDs directly has also emerged. Chen and Feng synthesized blue-emitting CDs with acrylic acid and 1,2-ethanediamine and combined with lanthanide complexes to prepare polymer composite film with poly(methyl methacrylate) as a matrix. By adjusting the ratio of CDs and lanthanide complexes, the polymer composite film emitted pure white light with a quantum efficiency of 16.6% under 400 nm laser [16]. This method first used CDs as color converters for white-light laser illumination, but lanthanide rare earth phosphors were needed. Therefore, using CDs as a single matrix color converter for laser illumination has become the key point to current research.

GQDs, as well-crystallized CDs [17], possess exceptional advantages such as high chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, and low toxicity [18,19]. Meanwhile, GQDs consist of several layers of sp2 hybridized carbon structure, which is beneficial to improving their stability [20]. Therefore, GQDs as color converters for white-light laser devices are highly expected to effectively reduce the cost of white-light laser illumination.

Herein, a research scheme of crystalline GQDs as color converter for white-light LD (Scheme 1 ) was presented. Two-step solvothermal method was utilized to fabricate the High-QY green-emitting GQDs, graphene oxide (GO) as the raw materials, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) as the nitrogen dopant, and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as the oxidant. Owing to the co-doping of nitrogen and oxygen, functional groups such as carbonyl and amides gradually are introduced, new surface states are formed in GQDs, and the electronic structure and energy state of GQDs are changed. More electrons are captured by the new surface states, which promotes the radiation recombination of electron-hole pairs and increases the QY of GQD solution to 47.09%. The GQD solution in DMF emits bright yellow light at a high concentration (6.0 mg mL−1). During the preparation of GQD fluorescent film, the silane coupling agent KH-792 undergoes a hydrolysis reaction to generate SiO2 network encapsulating GQDs. Finally, white light is realized by combining GQD film with a 405 nm laser diode. This work provides a novel solution for obtaining the fluorescent materials for promoting low-cost and high-efficiency white-light LDs.

Graphite powder, sodium nitrate, concentrated sulfuric acid, concentrated hydrochloric acid, and potassium permanganate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. DMF and H2O2 were purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. KH-792 was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. Dialysis bags with the retained molecular weight of 500 Da were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Rhodamine 6G was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used without further purification. Deionized water was self-made.

GO was obtained by a modified Hummer's method [21], more details is shown in SI. To prepare GQDs, GO (0.12 g) was added into 30 mL of DMF, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 50 min and then transferred to a autoclave, heated at 200 °C for 7 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally, 2 mL of H2O2 was injected and the mixed system was heated at 200 °C for 4 h. The obtained solution was light yellow.

The solution was filtered to remove the impurity and the vacuum distillation was then performed to remove DMF solvent. The remaining solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) for 4 days, the dialysis solution outside the dialysis bag was deionized water. Finally, the solid product was obtained through freeze-drying at −80 °C for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent film and LD devices
ce:section.ce:para: First, 6 mg of GQD solid powders was dissolved in 1 mL of DMF to form GQD solution with a concentration of 6.0 mg mL−1. Subsequently, 1 mL of KH-792 and 0.1 mL of deionized water were added to the GQD solution, and the mixed solution was uniformly dispersed by sonicating for 5 min. Finally, the solution was dropped into a mold, which was placed in a vacuum oven at 70 °C. After 12 h, a bright yellow-emitting GQD film was formed.

The laser diode was fixed on the test bench, and the positive and negative poles of the line were connected to an external power supply. The GQD film was fixed above the laser diode, and the lens of the test equipment was moved to aim at the laser device. Finally, the optical performance of the device was tested.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of GQDs was measured on an SPA-300 HV scanning probe microscope. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded on a DX-2700 diffractometer. Raman spectrum was measured on a HORIBA Evolution micro-Raman spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded with a BRUKER TENSOR 27 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and temperature dependent spectra were recorded on a Horiba Fluoromax-4 luminescence spectrometer. The fluorescence decay status was measured on an Edinburgh F980 spectrometer. TG curve was conducted on a Setaram Labsys Evo thermal analyzer. The property of LD device was tested by a PR-655 spectral scanning colorimeter.

The QY of GQDs was determined by a relative method. Specially, rhodamine 6G (QY = 95% in ethanol) was selected as a standard. First, UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorbance A of the standard solution and GQD solution at 420 nm. To avoid the influence of internal filtration effect on the absorption of sample, the A value was controlled below 0.1. Then, the PL spectra of four standard solutions and GQD solutions with different absorbances were measured at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm with a fluorescence spectrometer. Their integrated fluorescence intensities in the emission wavelength range of 440–700 nm were calculated. Finally, from the plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance, the QY of GQDs was calculated according to the following formula:
Q =
Q
s t
×
K X
K
s t
×
n X 2
n
s t
2
where Q is the QY of sample, K is the slope of the plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity versus the absorbance, and n is the refractive index of solvent (1.43 for DMF (n x) and 1.36 for ethanol (n st)). The subscripts, including x and st, refer to the sample and the standard, respectively.

TEM and AFM images indicated the morphology of GQDs, which are quasi-spherical and have uniform dispersibility without aggregation (Fig. 1 (a)). The particle sizes of GQDs are mainly distributed in 4–6.5 nm with an average particle size of 5.2 nm. The HRTEM images of GQDs (Fig. 1(a)) show obvious (100) lattice fringes corresponding to graphene with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm [22], which indicates GQDs have a high degree of crystallinity. The AFM image of GQDs in Fig. 1(b) shows that they are uniformly dispersed and their height profile is between 1.5 and 2 nm, indicating that most of GQDs primarily consist of 1–4 graphene layers [23].

To further investigate the structure and crystallinity of GQDs, XRD and Raman characterizations were performed. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the (002) lattice plane of graphite carbon at 22.6° was observed, indicating that GQDs have a graphitic structure and a certain degree of graphitization [23,24]. Fig. 2(b) is the Raman spectrum of GQDs. Two characteristic bands of graphite structure can be seen. The G band is derived from sp2 conjugated carbon or crystalline carbon, and the D band is derived from disordered carbon. The intensity ratio of G band to D band is 2.70, which shows that GQDs have a high degree of sp2 conjugation and graphitization [23], which is consistent with their HRTEM and XRD analysis results. A high crystallization degree of GQDs would suggest their high thermal stability, as will discussed below.

In the full-scan XPS spectra of GO (Fig. 3 (a)) there are two typical peaks of C1s (286.69 eV) and O1s (532.46 eV), while in the full-scan XPS spectra of GQDs there are three typical peaks, including C1s (284.79 eV), N1s (399.69 eV), and O1s (531.93 eV), with a nitrogen to carbon ratio (N/C) of 7.99%, indicating that GQDs have a certain amount of nitrogen doping. The strong C1s and O1s peaks indicate that GQDs are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen atoms, while the relatively weak N1s peak indicates new surface states for GQDs. From Fig. 3(b), the high-resolution C1s spectrum proves the existence of C═C (284.6 eV), C–N (285.0 eV), C–O (285.4 eV), and C═O (288.4 eV) [23,25]. The presence of C–N and C═O indicates that nitrogen and oxygen atoms are involved in the formation of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the presence of pyridine nitrogen (399.6 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (400.6 eV), and graphitic nitrogen (402.4 eV) in the high-resolution N1s spectrum indicates that nitrogen has been doped into GQDs through amidation reaction [26]. The C═O (531.7 eV) and C–O (532.4 eV) peaks in the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) prove the existence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [27], making GQDs have excellent aqueous solubility, which is beneficial to the hydrolysis reaction in the formation process of GQD silanization film. The SiO2 produced by the hydrolysis can enhance the thermal stability of fluorescent film.

Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of GO and GQDs, the bands at around 3408 and 3176 cm−1 are ascribed to the O–H and N–H groups, respectively. C–H asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations are observed at 2922 and 2850 cm−1, respectively. The bands at around 1720, 1648, 1430, and 1120 cm−1 correspond to the functional groups of C═O, C═C, C–N, and C–O–C, respectively [28]. The bands of C–N and C═O prove the formation of amide bonds, indicating that nitrogen atoms are doped into GQDs through an amidation reaction. The stretching vibration band of O–H indicates the existence of hydroxyl functional groups, which makes GQDs have excellent aqueous solubility. The analysis results are consistent with the XPS results of GQDs.

The optical properties are essential parameters to evaluate the performance of GQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs solution in Fig. 5 (a) shows two distinct excitonic absorption bands, centered at 270 nm and 342 nm, attributable to the π‒π* transition of C═C and n‒π* transition of C═O or C═N, respectively. The excitonic absorption band at 342 nm determines the source of the green fluorescence of GQDs [24]. It is worth noting that the small overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum and the emission spectrum of GQDs indicates their small self-absorption, which is of great benefit to the efficient fluorescence emission of GQDs [23].

Fig. 5(b) shows the emission spectra of GQD solution. As the excitation wavelength increases from 285 to 505 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission peak of GQDs red-shifts from 480 to 555 nm, showing obvious excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. When the excitation wavelength is 405 nm, the emission intensity of GQDs reaches the maximum with the emission peak at 501 nm. The inset photographs in Fig. 5(b) indicated that GQD solution emits light yellow under sunlight while it emits bright green fluorescence under UV lamp. The excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of GQDs may be dominated by different types of electron transitions between the graphene basal plane and different functional groups (carbonyl, hydroxyl, amide groups, etc.), which appears as multifluorescent centers [29].

Subsequently, the quantum yield, fluorescence stability, and fluorescence lifetime of GQD solution were characterized. Fig. 6 (a) shows the QY curve of GQD solution. The calculated QY value of GQD solution is 47.09%, which is higher than that of most GQDs synthesized by the top-down method, as summarized in Table S1. The high QY of GQDs in this work is mainly due to the co-doping effect of nitrogen and oxygen atoms, which causes new surface states, such as carbonyl and amide groups, on their surfaces, and thus changes the electronic structure and energy state of GQDs. The new surface state captures more electrons and promotes the radiation recombination of electron-hole pairs, which effectively improves the illumination brightness of LDs and provides a novel route for synthesizing high QY of GQDs by top-down method. In the field of laser illumination, since the color converters require high fluorescence stability, the photostability of GQDs was evaluated by UV irradiation (Fig. 6(b)), the light source is a 150 W ozone-free xenon lamp with the wavelength of 365 nm. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs keeps almost unchanged under irradiation lasting up to 60 min. Besides, after GQDs solution was stored at room temperature for ten months, they remained uniform and transparent with strong green fluorescence under UV lamp (Figure S1). These results all prove that GQDs have excellent fluorescence stability, which may be due to the high degree of crystallization of GQDs [30].

The time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of GQDs was fitted by a double exponential function (Fig. 6(c)), including τ 1 component (5.94 ns) and τ 2 component (16.4 ns), which account for 35.11% and 64.89%, respectively, indicating that fluorescence emission is determined by carbon-core state and surface state [31]. The average lifetime of GQDs is as long as 12.73 ns, which is beneficial to the application of laser devices.

To achieve white light illumination, green-emitting GQDs need to composite blue and red-emitting fluorescent materials. The fluorescence properties of different GQD concentrations in DMF solution were investigated, and it was found that GQDs show obvious concentration-dependent fluorescence characteristics.

When the concentration of GQD solution increases from 1.5 to 6.0 mg mL−1, the emission of GQD solution shifts from green-emitting to bright yellow-emitting at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the emission peaks of GQD solutions with concentrations of 1.5, 3.0, 4.5, and 6.0 mg mL−1 correspond to 500, 516, 526, and 555 nm, respectively. In other words, as the concentration of GQD solution increases gradually, the fluorescence emission peaks shift to longer wavelength. Fig. 7(b) shows the emission spectra of 6.0 mg mL−1 GQD solution at different excitation wavelengths. With the excitation wavelength increasing from 365 to 585 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission peak shifts from 555 to 621 nm. When the excitation wavelength is 505 nm, the emission peak is located at 574 nm with the maximum emission intensity. The inset in Fig. 7(b) shows that the GQD solution of 6.0 mg mL−1 is dark brown under sunlight and bright yellow-emitting under UV lamp. This concentration-dependent PL properties may be due to the fact that with the increase of GQD concentration, more GQDs are stacked together to expand the graphene basal plane, which has a smaller bandgap than a single quantum dot, leading to a redshift in GQD emission wavelength. Meanwhile, the interaction of functional groups (hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups, etc.) in the aggregated GQDs leads to the decrease in energy gap, which is also a factor leading to the redshift of emission wavelength [29].

The yellow-emitting 6.0 mg mL−1 GQD solution was mixed with KH-792 to form a bright yellow-emitting GQD fluorescent film. Fig. 7(c) shows the emission spectra of the fluorescent film at different excitation wavelengths. As the excitation wavelength increases from 365 to 585 nm, the fluorescence emission peak shifts from 531 to 620 nm, showing the same excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior as GQD solution state. From the inset in Fig. 7(c) of GQD fluorescent film under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp, it can be seen that the fluorescent film is dark brown and emits bright yellow light when excited by UV lamp.

Since the working temperature of laser devices is usually around 300 °C, the stability of GQD fluorescent film is essential to rationalize the feasibility of its application in laser illumination. Therefore, TG analysis was conducted to evaluate the thermostability of fluorescent film. Fig. 8 (a) shows a TG curve of GQD fluorescent film in the range of 100–800 °C. A weight loss of 2.62% at 300 °C suggests excellent thermal stability. On one hand, the high thermal stability of this fluorescent film depends on the high crystallization degree of GQDs. On the other hand, when KH-792 is mixed with GQD solution, it is hydrolyzed to produce SiO2. Thus, GQDs can be embedded in the highly stable SiO2 matrix, which dramatically improves the thermal stability of GQD fluorescent film [32]. The fluorescent stability of the film was evaluated by exposure to UV light for 60 min. As can be seen in Fig. 8(b), the PL intensity of GQD fluorescent film was almost unchanged and remained stable during irradiation, demonstrating excellent fluorescence stability of the film. In addition, the temperature dependent spectra of GQD fluorescent film under excitation at 405 nm were investigated. The film were heated from 25 to 250 °C with the interval of 25 °C and maintained at each temperature for 2 min. As shown in Fig. 8(c), when the temperature rises from 25 to 125 °C, the intensity of the emission spectra of GQD fluorescent film is almost unchanged. When the temperature increases from 150 to 200 °C, the intensity of emission spectra decreases slightly. From the normalized PL intensity of GQD fluorescent film in Fig. 8(d), it can be seen that the PL intensity at 200 °C drops to 90.7% of the initial intensity, and the GQD fluorescent film maintains 79.8% even at 250 °C, which illustrates the good optical stability of GQD fluorescent film for laser illumination.

According to the above analyses, GQD film has excellent fluorescence characteristics and high thermal stability. To explore the potential of GQD fluorescent film applied in laser illumination as a color converter, it was combined with a 405 nm LD to observe the luminescence of LD devices under different voltages. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), the narrow emission peak at 405 nm and the broad emission region from 450 to 700 nm are from the LDs and the yellow-emitting GQDs film, respectively. The inset in Fig. 9(a) shows the white-emitting LD device under the voltage of 6.6 V. It can be seen that the LD device emits bright white light. The CIE coordinates of this white light LD device is (0.34, 0.39) with the CCT of 5288 K and the CRI of 70 (Fig. 9(b)).

In summary, the green-emitting GQD solution with a high degree of crystallization, long lifetime (12.73 ns), and high QY (47.09%) were prepared by the two-step solvothermal method. The GQD solution emitted bright yellow light through simple concentration regulation. A highly stable yellow-emitting film was obtained by mixing the yellow GQDs with KH-792 for their embedment in SiO2 matrix. The weight loss of the film was only 2.62% at a high temperature of 300 °C, suggesting its good thermal stability. A white LD device, with CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.39), CCT of 5288 K, and CRI of 70, was realized by combining the yellow-emitting film as a single matrix color converter with LD. This research will open up an advanced application field of GQDs as laser fluorescent material, and provide a new solution for lasers to achieve white light illumination.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1710117, 51972221, U1610255), the Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2020-051, HGKY2019027), the Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology (201605D131045-10) and the Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Program (201603D111010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2021.118275.

Pinyi He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation. Su Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Xinghua Liu: Methodology, Software, Validation. Jingxia Zheng: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yongzhen Yang and Xuguang Liu: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Bingshe Xu: Resources, Research platform.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Al2O3-YAG:Ce composite phosphor ceramic: a thermally robust and efficient color converter for solid state laser lighting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Li, Q. Zhu, D. Tang, X. Liu, G. Ouyang, L. Cao, et al., Al2O3-YAG:Ce composite phosphor ceramic: a thermally robust and efficient color converter for solid state laser lighting, J. Mater. Chem. C 4 (37) (2016) 8648-8654. https://doi.org/10.1039/C6TC03215J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New insights into the microstructure of translucent CaAlSiN3:Eu2+ phosphor ceramics for solid-state laser lighting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Li, D. Tang, Z. Tian, X. Liu, T. Takeda, N. Hirosaki, et al., New insights into the microstructure of translucent CaAlSiN3:Eu2+ phosphor ceramics for solid-state laser lighting, J. Mater. Chem. C 5 (5) (2017) 1042-1051. https://doi.org/10.1039/C6TC04987G.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unique color converter architecture enabling phosphor-in-glass (PiG) films suitable for high-power and high-luminance laser-driven white lighting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Zheng, S. Li, L. Wang, T.L. Zhou, S. You, T. Takeda, et al., Unique color converter architecture enabling phosphor-in-glass (PiG) films suitable for high-power and high-luminance laser-driven white lighting, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 10 (17) (2018) 14930-14940. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.8b03168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Four-color laser white illuminant demonstrating high color-rendering quality

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Neumann, J.J. Wierer, W. Davis, Y. Ohno, S.R.J. Brueck, J.Y. Tsao, Four-color laser white illuminant demonstrating high color-rendering quality, Opt. Express 19 (14) (2011) A982-A990. https://doi.org/10.1364/OE.19.00A982.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A search for extra-high brightness laser-driven color converters by investigating thermally-induced luminance saturation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xu, S. Li, P. Zheng, L. Wang, S. You, T. Takeda, et al., A search for extra-high brightness laser-driven color converters by investigating thermally-induced luminance saturation, J. Mater. Chem. C 7 (37) (2019) 11449-11456. https://doi.org/10.1039/C9TC03919H.
